content.claim: Academic literature on cross-country education systems documents that early tracking increases educational inequality and tends to reduce mean performance; Hungary's PISA data show a 170-point reading gap between the most and least advantaged decile (roughly four years of schooling), consistent with these effects.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Political feasibility lens
limitations: The literature on early tracking's effects is general; attribution of Hungary's specific inequality gap to the 6/8-year track system specifically, rather than to other selective mechanisms or broader socioeconomic factors, is not established in the sources reviewed
No source isolates the incremental effect of removing 6/8-year tracks on Hungary's overall inequality gap
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
